seoTitle: How to Zoom In and Out in Microsoft Outlook
metaDescription: Fix tiny text in Outlook using the Ctrl plus scroll wheel shortcut, the ribbon zoom slider, and a permanent zoom setting that sticks across restarts.
intro: Squinting at small email text shouldn't be part of your daily routine. This guide covers the fast Ctrl plus scroll wheel shortcut for reading messages, the ribbon-based zoom tool, and how to lock in a permanent zoom level so it persists every time you open Outlook.

steps.heading: Zoom with your scroll wheel
steps.body: Open a message pane, hold Ctrl, and scroll your mouse wheel up to zoom in or down to zoom out instantly.

steps.heading: Use the ribbon zoom tool
steps.body: Double-click an email to open it in its own window, go to the Message tab, and click the Zoom button in the ribbon.

steps.heading: Pick a custom percentage
steps.body: Select a preset zoom level or type a custom value and click OK to apply it.

steps.heading: Set a permanent global zoom
steps.body: From the main application window, go to the View tab and open the Zoom command in the Layout tools section.

steps.heading: Save your preference
steps.body: Choose your desired scale, such as 125% or 150%, and check the box to remember your preference before clicking OK.

steps.heading: Verify it sticks
steps.body: Open other unread messages to confirm the zoom level persists across your inbox.

faq.question: Why does my Outlook zoom reset every time I open a new email?
faq.answer: If you haven't checked the "remember my preference" option in the global Zoom settings, your changes may only apply temporarily.

faq.question: Is there a keyboard shortcut for zooming instead of scrolling?
faq.answer: The Ctrl plus scroll wheel combination is the fastest built-in shortcut, though you can also use the ribbon's Zoom button for precise percentages.

faq.question: Does zooming affect how my emails print?
faq.answer: Zoom settings control your on-screen reading view and don't change the print layout or formatting of your emails.

faq.question: Can I set a different zoom level for composing versus reading?
faq.answer: Yes, the composition window has its own zoom scaling separate from the reading pane's magnification level.
